speaker: interviewee
text: Then now on a ship.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job on the ship as a dishwasher. However, I cannot stand-- I have sea sickness. And I cannot be on the ship. I went on the ship, we went-- we made two trips to France from Israel, and let me point out that it was a great job, great food, great job. And who could have asked for something more? We went twice to France to pick up [NON-ENGLISH], people to Israel-- that came to Israel. However, during the time I was supposed to be working and dish washing, and washing dishes. All I had to do was just run to the bathroom, and from the top, in a-- I just could not stand. And I knew that eventually they will let me go, because I did not perform my work. During the two trips, I went twice to France and back, I just could not perform the work. So I decided that on the third trip, because as I mentioned, I did not have a passport. And I-- without a passport, no country will let you in. You cannot get a visa. On the third trip to France, I jumped ship in Marseille, and I said goodbye to Israel. I never came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Marseille I drove-- I had some money. I went to Paris. From Paris, I went to a Jewish Committee there. To a-- rather, to a Jewish restaurant there. And they supplied me with a fellow who smuggled me over the border from France back to Germany. Because I knew that if I want to go some-- I couldn't stay in France. I had no passport, I was illegal. And I don't know the language, and I don't have a trade. What am I going to do in France? And I knew that if I go back to Germany, from Germany, I have a chance to go anyplace that they will take me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went back to Germany, and-- let me point out, when I came back to Germany, I could not get my job back in the Jewish Committee. In order stay there and to make a living, I bought a car. And I was driving as a taxi. Not official-- as an official taxi, but from the Jewish Committee, with Jewish people to their homes street, to the rail station, to there. And this is how I made a living. And while I was in Munich there, I registered to Canada, to Australia, to South Africa, to South America, to America. And I figured, whoever is going to give me first the visa, whoever will take me first, that's where I will go. Now this was in 1950. In 1951, I received a note from the Jewish Committee, from the AJDC, the American Jewish Joint Distribution Committee, that I should come in for an interview. And that day, I was granted a visa to go to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow.

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- the [NON-ENGLISH] took care of it. And I went, I sold my car, and I had some money, and I came to the United States. The [NON-ENGLISH] paid for the trip, probably. Although I came on a military ship. I came on a military ship. And we came-- I came, I landed in the United States on February 19, 1951.

speaker: interviewer
text: New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: In New York. However, I did not see anything from New York. I landed in New York, and the Jewish family in children service, who gave me an affidavit was from Minneapolis. The same day that I arrived on the ship, they put me on a railroad, and they transport, and I drove to-- to Minneapolis. Now, being green as I was, they gave me a sticker that said 'New American.' But I didn't want to stand out of the crowd, because everyone else didn't wear one. So I took that-- that button off, and I put it in my pocket. And I did not know English, just a few words. But hardly any. And on the train I met another Jewish fellow, and either he noticed me that I took it off, or he knew, and we started to talk. And he knew a little bit Jewish, a little bit German. And-- and we got to talking, he said, well, he is going to Saint Paul. And Saint Paul, Minneapolis, Saint Paul is-- are twin cities, almost the same. Just across-- one is across the Mississippi from the-- from the other one. And we arrived at night, we arrived all in the dark. This was now probably about 6:00, 7:00 in February. And they were supposed to pick me up. However, they were looking for someone who had wore a button, 'New American.' And I was the only one on the train to Minneapolis. They couldn't find anyone, and I didn't know about it. And we didn't meet. They went home, and I got stranded all by myself on the thing, with the Jewish fellow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyhow, the Jewish fellow said, well, we have to do something about it. He called the Jewish family service in Minneapolis, and in Saint Paul, and they said, well, we sent out someone there, and no one was there. So how come? Well, it was my fault that I didn't-- I didn't follow the orders. Did you finally connect? I finally connect. They came, and-- They came to the station, and they took me to-- they boarded me at the Jewish family. At the Jewish family, a very, very, fine family. As a matter of fact their name was Merowitz. The man was a-- he was a-- a sheriff. He was not sheriff, he was a Deputy Sheriff. He wore a gun, and he worked in the courthouse. Now, did all this work out for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It all worked out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got a job?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them for a whole year. I stayed with them for a whole year. Because the Jewish Family Service told me that they are responsible for me for at least for one year, if not for five.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got a job doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a job there. And I got a job as a cutter in a garment factory.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after a year, when the year was up, I said, Minneapolis is a beautiful city, and lovely people, but the weather is something else. So while I was in Minneapolis, I met some people that they were from Los Angeles. And they said, Los Angeles is constant, all the summer, and it's a beautiful country. When you are done, why don't you come over to Los Angeles? And as the year was up, I moved with my package, my suitcase to Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was-- actually, this was 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in the latter part of 1952. I met my wife in a Jewish center. At a Jewish center dance, I met my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you got a job in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a job in Los Angeles, in a furniture concern.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live in Los Angeles?

speaker: interviewee
text: First I lived at those people that I met in Minneapolis. And later after I got married, I got a job-- I got a job as an apprentice cutter, upholstery cutter.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that--

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were Jewish, too, and they were quite, very nice. And I got a nice wage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was that the beginning of your work life? You continued in this career?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had-- did that work out for you as a good way to earn a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I cannot complain. As you can see, I am-- I am not-- [NON-ENGLISH], but I cannot complain. I cannot complain. America was quite good for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you met your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met my wife in 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Los Angeles. And we got married in 1953. Well, the marriage, is a-- it was a funny part about it, how green we were. How I-- [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she an American?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, my wife is Jewish, but she is from Germany. My wife is from Germany, and she was-- during the war, she was in England. And we met, and we got married in 1953.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have two children. In 1954, we had our eldest son, Jack. And in 1955, we had our second son, Maurice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have some grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yes, Maurice has two lovely grandchildren, two lovely grandson. Two, yeah. One is Ben Jay, and the other one is Jeffrey Michael. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And are you retired now from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I am retired.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upholstery business?

speaker: interviewee
text: I am retired, yeah, for the past 10 years, I'm retired. And my sons, one son works here in the vicinity, and the other son is a teacher in Northern California.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to ask you one philosophical question to complete this part of our interview. You've told us a long story, and you had a lot of loss, and many, many really awful things happened to you, but you survived and lived a long life. What do you carry with you from those bad times? What did that leave, and how did it affect you the rest of your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people don't carry any hate. Now unfortunately, unfortunately, I must say I do. I do carry a hate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how does that affect you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot, I cannot be friendly on a real basis with a German, or a Pole. Now with the Germans, you can understand. Now the Poles, because they had no reason. The Germans, [NON-ENGLISH]. But the Poles, the anti-Semitism in Poland had no reason, and no validity. And they did it just out of meanness. So unfortunately, as I said, some people are very, very forgiving. And they-- unfortunately, I cannot count myself, I think, to the forgiveness.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that affect the way you view your religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, probably very much. I'm probably bitter. And I probably cannot make friends very easily. I probably-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] am suspicious. If someone is-- maybe someone is friendly, well, suspicious. You know, maybe he has-- he has something up his sleeve, you know? If some people, you know, there are some people that they're real nice, that they are really not out to get you, or to get-- to get the better of you. But unfortunately, this is.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to ask you to stop talking now, and then we will share your pictures, and meet Mrs. Fasenfest.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my mother. She was born, probably in 1918. I'm not sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think she was born-- do you think this picture was taken in--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this picture was probably taken at, the-- I would estimate at the age 40. Which was probably be in 1920's, or in the late 1920's.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Miriam [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the town where you think it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe she was born in Brzeziny. Brzeziny is near Lodz, Poland. This is a certificate that I received from the UNRRA. UNRRA is the United Nations Refugee Resettlement Association. I received this certificate in July 7, 1945. This is a picture of me in my so-called uniform. And this is the original uniform that I wore during my years in the concentration camp. The picture is taken two months after I was liberated, so of course I ate my full at that time, and I don't look as starved as I was during the Reich times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Munich. And give your name, please. My name is Sam J. Fasenfest. Here it is marked down as Fasenfest Schlioma. And there also is-- I have also marked down there my concentration camp number from Dachau. It is 95335. This is a picture of me. I am the third from the left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Sam J. Fasenfest. And this was taken in Israel in the latter part of 1948. And this was on a military base. There I was instructed, so to say, to be a soldier. In a military base in Israel. This is a wedding picture of my eldest son, Jack Maxine. It was taken in 1987, in San Mateo.

speaker: interviewer
text: California?

speaker: interviewee
text: San Mateo, California. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name of his wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: His wife's name is Julie.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you give their last name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name is Lou. L-O-U.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now she's Fasenfest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now she is Fasenfest, of course. This is a picture of my youngest son, Maurice [NON-ENGLISH], with his wife. His wife's name is Ellen Phillips. And they have two lovely boys. Ben JAY, and Geoffrey Michael Fasenfest.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture was taken in 1984, I believe. And it was taken in Etna, California, where they live.

speaker: interviewer
text: You look handsome, both of you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Give us a signal when to start. [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: I would like to introduce my wife, Irma Ilsa Fasenfest. We met in 1952, and we got married in 1953. I have a little funny story I want to tell you about how we got married, because, it's really funny. When I asked my wife to marry me, and she agreed, we went to the city hall, and we got our license, and we got our blood samples, and we went home. And we were-- and we thought that we were married already. It didn't dawn on us until about 14 days later that someone asked to show-- that we should show them the license, that we were not married. We were not married at all. We'd thought we were married, but all we had was just the license. We had to go back to a judge, and the judge married us on July the 6th, 1953. It is funny that we were-- we thought that we are married and, we weren't. This is why, up till today, we are not sure of our wedding date. We don't know if the wedding date is when we took out the license, or we really got married. My wife finally decided that we will have our wedding day on her mother's birthday. Which is--

speaker: interviewee
text: A little bit before, when we took out the license.

speaker: interviewee
text: So up till now, this is how we-- how we have our wedding anniversaries.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, too, am a survivor. I was a raised and lived in Germany. And 1948, I emigrated to England, and from there to Scotland, where my sister went first.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: '38, I went to London. And then from London I went to Scotland, where my sister lived. She had gone before me. And my brother came later on, also. And I worked there for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seven years as a domestic. That was the only way we could get into England. And took care of children, cleaned homes. And then afterwards, for two years, I worked for my brother in his photo studio. And because towards the end of the war, they gave us permission to do something different. And then from there, I went back to Germany and worked for one year, for the American Civil Service there, doing censoring German mail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you meet Sam at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't meet him. Not yet. I met him-- emigrate, I liked it very much working for the Americans, and so I wanted to come to America. And I came here, and I met Sam in Los Angeles, and we got married. And that's about it. And here we are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I would like to thank you very much for letting us explain everything in our-- what we went through. And especially for the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sam went through much more than I did.

speaker: interviewee
text: For the history, and I hope that some people will realize how it was, and how we suffered, and so forth.
